Low-level design interview prep LLD

Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → LLD foundations and prompt framing → Validation, errors, and state transitions

Lesson 3 of 3

Validation, errors, and state transitions

Design methods that enforce invariants, express failure paths cleanly, and keep state changes easy to reason about.

25-35 min LLD flow LLD foundations and prompt framing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Low-level design quality shows up in how safely objects move between states, reject invalid commands, and explain failure without leaking inconsistent state.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Model valid transitions Name the lifecycle states explicitly2. Choose an error strategy Reserve exceptions for truly exceptional control flow when possible3. Protect invariants under change Validate before persisting or publishing side effects

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Low-level design quality shows up in how safely objects move between states, reject invalid commands, and explain failure without leaking inconsistent state. 2. Model valid transitions Name the lifecycle states explicitly3. Choose an error strategy Reserve exceptions for truly exceptional control flow when possible4. Protect invariants under change Validate before persisting or publishing side effects
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Low-level design quality shows up in how safely objects move between states, reject invalid commands, and explain failure without leaking inconsistent state.

What to say first

  • Describe the legal state machine for the core object.
  • Show how invalid commands are rejected.
  • Keep state mutation and validation in the same abstraction.
2

Study section

Model valid transitions

Document which operations are legal in each state so the object graph cannot drift into combinations the business rules do not allow.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the lifecycle states explicitly
  • Check transitions before mutating dependent fields
  • Keep derived status consistent with source data
3

Study section

Choose an error strategy

Use exceptions, result objects, or domain-specific failures consistently so callers know whether an operation can be retried, corrected, or must abort.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Reserve exceptions for truly exceptional control flow when possible
  • Expose domain failures in terms the caller can act on
  • Make partial success impossible unless it is explicitly modeled
4

Study section

Protect invariants under change

Guard methods at the boundary and centralize critical validation so follow-up requirements do not scatter rule checks across unrelated classes.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Validate before persisting or publishing side effects
  • Keep invariant logic close to the state it protects
  • Avoid setter-heavy designs that let callers bypass rules

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Describe the legal state machine for the core object.
  • Show how invalid commands are rejected.
  • Keep state mutation and validation in the same abstraction.
  • Explain how callers observe and handle domain errors.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Updating several fields independently without an invariant boundary.
  • Mixing technical exceptions with domain-level outcomes inconsistently.
  • Exposing public setters that let callers skip business rules.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you model state changes for an order or booking lifecycle?
  • When is a result object cleaner than throwing an exception?
  • How do you stop invariant checks from being duplicated everywhere?

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Validation, errors, and state transitions" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you model state changes for an order or booking lifecycle?
  • Model valid transitions
  • Choose an error strategy
  • Describe the legal state machine for the core object.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
